case_name: Ramesh Kumar Sharma vs Star Health and Allied Insurance Co. Ltd.
issue: Star Health rejected a cashless claim for knee replacement surgery, citing that the patient's knee degeneration was a pre-existing disease within the 4-year PED waiting period. The policyholder had disclosed mild knee pain at inception but was told by the agent it would be covered after 2 years.
ruling: The Insurance Ombudsman ruled in favor of the complainant. The insurer failed to establish that the condition at the time of surgery was directly continuous from the disclosed knee pain at inception. The Ombudsman directed Star Health to pay the full claim amount of ₹3.2 lakh within 30 days.
principle: The burden of proving that a current medical condition is a continuation of a pre-existing disease lies with the insurer. Medical evidence must establish a causal and continuous link between the condition at inception and the current claim. Agent's verbal assurances, while not legally binding, demonstrate the insurer's representatives misled the consumer.
consumer_takeaway: If your claim is rejected as PED, ask the insurer to provide the specific medical evidence linking your current condition to the alleged pre-existing disease. If they cannot establish a clear medical link, challenge the rejection. Keep all correspondence from agents in writing.
reference: Insurance Ombudsman, Bangalore, Award No. BLR-H-023-2021-22

case_name: Priya Venkataraman vs HDFC ERGO General Insurance Co. Ltd.
issue: HDFC ERGO denied a cashless pre-authorization for planned coronary bypass surgery on the grounds that the request was submitted 24 hours (not 48 hours) in advance of the planned admission date. The policyholder argued that the surgery was medically urgent and could not be planned further in advance.
ruling: The District Consumer Disputes Redressal Commission ruled in favor of the complainant. The Commission held that when a planned surgery is medically urgent and the doctor certified urgency, requiring strict 48-hour pre-authorization is an unreasonable condition that defeats the purpose of health insurance. HDFC ERGO was directed to pay the claim of ₹5.8 lakh with 9% interest from the date of claim and ₹50,000 in compensation.
principle: Procedural requirements for pre-authorization cannot be enforced rigidly when the medical urgency of a procedure is established by a qualified physician. Insurers cannot use technical grounds to deny valid medical claims when the insured has acted in good faith and medical necessity is documented.
consumer_takeaway: If your cashless request is denied citing a procedural technicality (timing, format), obtain a medical urgency certificate from your doctor immediately. File a complaint with the insurer's grievance cell citing the urgency. If denied, escalate to the Ombudsman or Consumer Forum — procedural technicalities rarely justify full claim denial.
reference: District Consumer Forum, Chennai, Case No. CC/234/2022

case_name: Suresh Babu vs Care Health Insurance Ltd.
issue: Care Health Insurance rejected a reimbursement claim for hospitalization of the policyholder's 68-year-old mother for treatment of Type 2 diabetes-related complications, citing non-disclosure of the pre-existing diabetic condition at the time of adding the mother as a dependent under the family floater policy.
ruling: The Insurance Ombudsman found that while the mother had diabetes, the policyholder had disclosed it on the proposal form. Care Health's underwriting team had accepted the proposal with a specific exclusion endorsement for diabetes complications for 4 years. However, the exclusion endorsement was never communicated to the policyholder in writing. The Ombudsman held that exclusions imposed after proposal acceptance must be explicitly communicated and accepted by the insured. Directed full payment of ₹1.8 lakh.
principle: Any endorsements or exclusions added to a health policy (beyond the standard terms) must be explicitly communicated to the policyholder in writing and the policyholder must be given the opportunity to accept or exercise the free look period. Silent exclusions are not enforceable.
consumer_takeaway: At policy inception, if you have pre-existing conditions, ask the insurer in writing: 'What are the exact endorsements and exclusions on my policy?' Get the policy schedule with any exclusion endorsements. Use the 30-day free look period to review — if there are unacceptable exclusions, return the policy.
reference: Insurance Ombudsman, Mumbai, Award No. MUM-H-019-2022-23

case_name: Anita Desai vs Bajaj Allianz General Insurance Co. Ltd.
issue: Bajaj Allianz reduced the reimbursement claim amount from ₹4.2 lakh to ₹2.8 lakh citing proportionate deduction due to room rent exceeding the policy sub-limit of ₹2,500/day. The policyholder had been allocated a ₹5,000/day room by the hospital due to non-availability of lower-category rooms.
ruling: The State Consumer Disputes Redressal Commission held that when a lower-category room is not available in the hospital and the policyholder did not voluntarily upgrade, the proportionate deduction clause cannot be applied. The room allocation by the hospital's administration does not constitute voluntary choice of a higher room. Bajaj Allianz was directed to pay the balance ₹1.4 lakh with interest.
principle: The proportionate deduction clause in health insurance applies when the insured voluntarily chooses a higher room category than their policy entitlement. When the hospital allocates a higher-cost room due to unavailability of lower rooms, the proportionate deduction should not apply — the insured must be given the benefit of doubt.
consumer_takeaway: If you are allocated a higher room due to unavailability of lower rooms, get a written certificate from the hospital's administration stating that no lower-category room was available at the time of admission. This certificate is your defense against proportionate deduction. Always ask for this document proactively.
reference: State Consumer Forum, Maharashtra, Case No. FA/112/2021

case_name: Mohammed Irfan vs New India Assurance Co. Ltd.
issue: New India Assurance rejected a reimbursement claim for domiciliary treatment of the policyholder's elderly father who was being treated for severe COPD at home. The insurer argued that the condition did not meet the criteria for domiciliary treatment as hospitalization was available.
ruling: The Insurance Ombudsman upheld the complainant's case. The treating physician had certified that given the patient's advanced age (82 years), severe respiratory compromise, and risk of hospital-acquired infection, domiciliary treatment was medically preferable and constituted a valid case for domiciliary coverage. Directed New India to pay ₹85,000 for the domiciliary treatment costs.
principle: Domiciliary treatment eligibility is not limited only to situations where hospital beds are unavailable. A qualified physician's medical judgment that home treatment is preferable due to patient condition and risk factors is a valid ground for domiciliary treatment coverage.
consumer_takeaway: For domiciliary claims, ensure your doctor's certificate specifically explains the medical reasoning — not just 'patient is elderly' but the specific clinical risks of hospitalization vs home treatment. The certificate should cite the patient's specific condition, comorbidities, and why home treatment is medically indicated.
reference: Insurance Ombudsman, Hyderabad, Award No. HYD-H-041-2021-22

case_name: Kavitha Rao vs Niva Bupa Health Insurance Co. Ltd.
issue: Niva Bupa denied portability from a previous insurer (Bajaj Allianz) by claiming underwriting risk, even though the policyholder had 5 continuous years of coverage and was entitled to portability credit for a served PED waiting period for hypertension.
ruling: The Insurance Ombudsman found that Niva Bupa's denial of portability on grounds of underwriting risk was not in accordance with IRDAI Portability Guidelines 2011. The insurer must accept portability for the same sum insured and cannot use portability as a new proposal for fresh underwriting. Directed Niva Bupa to issue the policy with full portability credit for the 5-year PED waiting period served.
principle: Under IRDAI Portability Guidelines, an insurer cannot refuse portability on health grounds for the same or lower sum insured. The porting insurer must credit all waiting periods served with the previous insurer. Rejection of portability citing underwriting risk is not a valid ground and violates IRDAI regulations.
consumer_takeaway: If an insurer refuses to accept your portability request, report it to IRDAI immediately — this is a regulatory violation. File on the IRDAI Bima Bharosa portal citing 'Portability denial' and provide your previous insurer's renewal certificate showing the number of continuous years. IRDAI takes these violations seriously.
reference: Insurance Ombudsman, New Delhi, Award No. DEL-H-007-2022-23

case_name: Harish Patel vs Oriental Insurance Co. Ltd.
issue: Oriental Insurance denied a hospitalization claim for mental illness treatment (schizophrenia), stating that mental illness is not covered under the standard health policy. The policyholder argued that IRDAI and the Mental Healthcare Act 2017 mandate coverage for mental illness.
ruling: The District Consumer Forum ruled strongly in favor of the complainant. The ruling cited Section 21(4) of the Mental Healthcare Act, 2017 and the IRDAI circular (July 2020) directing all insurers to cover mental illness on the same basis as physical illness. Oriental Insurance was directed to pay the claim of ₹1.1 lakh with 12% interest from the date of claim and ₹1 lakh in punitive compensation for willful non-compliance with statutory mandates.
principle: Mental illness coverage is a statutory right under the Mental Healthcare Act, 2017 and IRDAI regulations. An insurer's policy exclusion for mental illness is contrary to law and cannot be enforced. The award of punitive compensation is appropriate when the insurer knowingly violates statutory provisions.
consumer_takeaway: Mental illness hospitalization MUST be covered by your health insurer. If rejected, cite specifically: (1) Mental Healthcare Act 2017, Section 21(4), (2) IRDAI Circular ref. IRDAI/HLT/REG/CIR/2020 on mental illness coverage. File complaints with IRDAI Bima Bharosa — this is a clear regulatory violation that warrants immediate intervention.
reference: District Consumer Forum, Ahmedabad, Case No. CC/87/2020

case_name: Geeta Krishnamurthy vs Star Health and Allied Insurance Co. Ltd.
issue: Star Health cancelled the health insurance policy of the complainant citing non-disclosure of a previous policy rejection by another insurer. The policyholder argued she was not aware that a rejection needed to be disclosed and that the condition was not related to any claim.
ruling: The High Court held that while the obligation to disclose material facts is broad, not all previous policy rejections constitute a material fact unless the rejection was for a health-related reason that would affect the current insurer's risk assessment. A rejection for administrative reasons or unrelated factors does not require disclosure. The court directed reinstatement of the policy with no retrospective claim denial.
principle: Material non-disclosure must relate to facts that would materially affect the insurer's decision to accept or price the risk. Previous policy rejections are material facts only if the rejection was for health/risk-related reasons. Administrative rejections or unrelated rejections do not automatically constitute material non-disclosure.
consumer_takeaway: If you have had a previous policy rejection, disclose it in your new proposal — but also mention the reason for rejection. If the rejection was for administrative or unrelated reasons, clearly explain this. Hiding previous rejections is risky — but an insurer cannot cancel a policy citing 'previous rejection' without establishing materiality to the current risk.
reference: Madras High Court, Writ Petition No. 12847/2021

case_name: Ramachandran Pillai vs United India Insurance Co. Ltd.
issue: United India Insurance refused to renew the complainant's health policy without explanation. The policyholder had made two claims in the previous year totaling ₹4.5 lakh. The insurer cited underwriting decision but provided no written reason.
ruling: The Insurance Ombudsman directed United India Insurance to provide a written reasoned decision for the renewal refusal. The Ombudsman held that while insurers have discretion in renewal, they must provide a reasoned decision in writing. The blanket refusal to renew was found to be arbitrary. Directed to offer a renewal with appropriate risk-based premium adjustment if warranted, rather than outright denial.
principle: While insurers have the right to decline renewal, they must act fairly and transparently. A renewal denial without written, specific reasons constitutes arbitrary conduct. IRDAI regulations require insurers to act fairly in the renewal process, particularly for policyholders who have been loyal customers for extended periods.
consumer_takeaway: If your health insurance renewal is denied, demand a written explanation citing specific reasons. An unexplained denial may be challengeable. If the denial is due to claims history, you may have the right to renewal with a premium loading but not outright denial after extended coverage.
reference: Insurance Ombudsman, Kochi, Award No. KOC-H-015-2022-23

case_name: Meenakshi Sundaram vs HDFC ERGO General Insurance Co. Ltd.
issue: HDFC ERGO denied a day care procedure claim for chemotherapy treatment, citing that the treatment lasted only 4 hours and did not meet the minimum 24-hour hospitalization requirement.
ruling: The Consumer Forum ruled entirely in favor of the complainant. The Forum cited IRDAI's mandatory list of Day Care Treatments (including chemotherapy) and held that 24-hour hospitalization is explicitly NOT required for listed day care procedures. HDFC ERGO was directed to pay the claim of ₹2.8 lakh for 4 chemotherapy sessions with 9% interest and ₹25,000 in compensation for deficiency in service.
principle: Denial of a day care claim listed in IRDAI's standard day care procedures list on grounds of 'less than 24 hours' hospitalization is contrary to IRDAI regulations. IRDAI has explicitly waived the 24-hour requirement for approved day care treatments, and an insurer cannot impose this condition for listed procedures.
consumer_takeaway: Chemotherapy, dialysis, cataract, lithotripsy, and other IRDAI-listed day care procedures are covered even without 24-hour hospitalization. If your day care claim is rejected citing 'less than 24 hours', this is a clear regulatory violation. File an immediate complaint with IRDAI Bima Bharosa — these cases are quickly resolved in the consumer's favor.
reference: State Consumer Disputes Redressal Commission, Tamil Nadu, Case No. FA/56/2021

case_name: Srinivasan Gopalan vs Max Life Insurance Co. Ltd.
issue: Max Life rejected a death claim citing non-disclosure of the deceased's history of depression at the time of taking the term plan 5 years ago. The policyholder had disclosed diabetes and hypertension but did not disclose the depression diagnosis from 7 years prior.
ruling: The Insurance Ombudsman ruled in favor of the complainant (deceased's family). The Ombudsman invoked Section 45 of the Insurance Act — as the policy was more than 3 years old at the time of death, Max Life cannot repudiate the claim on grounds of non-disclosure (except in cases of fraud, which was not established). The insurer's right to contest expired after 3 years. Directed Max Life to pay the full death benefit of ₹75 lakh.
principle: Section 45 of the Insurance Act 1938 (as amended) bars an insurer from repudiating a life insurance claim after 3 years from the date of policy commencement on grounds of misrepresentation or non-disclosure. This is a fundamental protection for policyholders — the insurer must contest within 3 years or lose the right to do so.
consumer_takeaway: This is the most important protection for life insurance nominees: if the policy has been in force for more than 3 years, the insurer CANNOT reject the claim for non-disclosure (unless fraud is proven). If your claim is rejected citing 'non-disclosure' on a policy older than 3 years, this rejection is likely illegal under Section 45.
reference: Insurance Ombudsman, Chennai, Award No. CHN-L-032-2021-22

case_name: Rajni Devi vs LIC of India
issue: LIC delayed settlement of a death claim for 8 months citing 'investigation' even though the deceased had held the policy for 7 years and died of natural causes in a hospital. The nominee (widow) with no income faced financial hardship.
ruling: The National Consumer Disputes Redressal Commission (NCDRC) condemned LIC's unreasonable delay. The NCDRC noted that IRDAI regulations mandate claim settlement within 30 days (non-investigated) and 120 days (investigated). An 8-month delay with no substantive investigation findings constitutes deficiency in service. LIC was directed to pay the full claim of ₹20 lakh with 12% interest from the date of claim registration and ₹2 lakh compensation to the widow.
principle: IRDAI mandates life insurance death claim settlement within 30 days of complete document receipt for non-investigated claims, and within 120 days for investigated claims. Unreasonable delays without substantive investigation constitute deficiency in service and attract compensation. Financial hardship of the nominee is a relevant factor in awarding compensation.
consumer_takeaway: IRDAI mandates clear timelines for death claim settlement. If LIC or any insurer delays beyond 30 days (or 120 days for investigations), file a formal complaint immediately. Delay in settlement is grounds for claiming interest and compensation. Keep records of all claim submission dates and insurer communications.
reference: NCDRC, New Delhi, Case No. II/2021

case_name: Vijaykumar Nair vs ICICI Prudential Life Insurance Co. Ltd.
issue: A life insurance agent sold ICICI Prudential's ULIP plan to a 65-year-old retired government employee, misrepresenting it as a 'risk-free fixed deposit alternative with guaranteed returns.' The policyholder invested ₹15 lakh and discovered after 2 years that the ULIP had market risk and no guaranteed returns.
ruling: The District Consumer Forum held that the agent's misrepresentation constituted mis-selling. The Forum ordered ICICI Prudential to refund the full premium of ₹15 lakh with 8% annual interest from the date of investment, plus ₹1 lakh in compensation and ₹50,000 in litigation costs. The Forum noted that selling a market-linked ULIP as a 'guaranteed FD' to a risk-averse senior citizen is an egregious violation of consumer rights.
principle: Insurance agents are representatives of the insurer — the insurer is vicariously liable for mis-selling by its agents. Selling a market-linked product by misrepresenting it as 'guaranteed returns' constitutes mis-selling and deceptive trade practice. IRDAI's 'Suitability' guidelines require insurers to assess whether the product matches the customer's risk profile and needs.
consumer_takeaway: If you were misled into buying an insurance product: (1) Invoke the 30-day free look period if still available, (2) File a formal written complaint with the insurer's grievance cell documenting exactly what was said by the agent, (3) Escalate to Ombudsman/Consumer Forum citing mis-selling. Always ask for the product brochure in writing before signing — verbal promises are not binding but documented mis-selling evidence supports your case.
reference: District Consumer Forum, Thiruvananthapuram, Case No. CC/190/2020

case_name: Sudheer Malhotra vs HDFC Life Insurance Co. Ltd.
issue: HDFC Life denied a death claim citing that the policyholder died by suicide within the first year of the policy, but the nominee disputed the suicide classification — the medical evidence was ambiguous and the death could have been accidental.
ruling: The Insurance Ombudsman held that the burden of proving suicide lies with the insurer. In cases of ambiguous cause of death, the benefit of doubt should be given to the nominee. The insurer could not establish beyond reasonable doubt that the death was by suicide. Directed HDFC Life to pay the full death benefit of ₹1 crore to the nominee.
principle: When the cause of death is ambiguous (between accidental death and suicide), the burden of proving suicide lies with the insurer. The insurer must establish suicide to a high degree of certainty using medical, forensic, and circumstantial evidence. Benefit of doubt in ambiguous cases goes to the nominee.
consumer_takeaway: If your life insurance claim is rejected citing 'suicide' but the death was ambiguous or could be accidental, challenge the classification. The insurer must produce specific evidence proving suicide — the investigating officer's opinion alone is not sufficient. An independent medico-legal opinion can be sought to counter the insurer's position.
reference: Insurance Ombudsman, Delhi, Award No. DEL-L-009-2022-23

case_name: Lalitha Krishnan vs SBI Life Insurance Co. Ltd.
issue: SBI Life Insurance denied the maturity claim of an endowment policy citing that the policy had lapsed 3 years before maturity and was converted to paid-up status. The policyholder was not informed about the paid-up conversion and expected the full maturity amount.
ruling: The Consumer Forum found that SBI Life had failed to communicate the policy lapse and paid-up conversion through the registered address — the communications went to an old address. Since SBI Life had the policyholder's updated contact details in their records, the failure to notify the policyholder about lapse and paid-up options constituted service deficiency. Directed SBI Life to pay the full maturity amount rather than the paid-up value.
principle: Insurers have a duty to communicate important policy changes (lapse, paid-up conversion, surrender value availability) to the policyholder at their registered/current contact details. Failure to communicate when the insurer has updated contact information is a service deficiency that can nullify the insurer's defense of technical policy conditions.
consumer_takeaway: Always keep your address, mobile number, and email updated with your life insurer. Send an email/letter to the insurer when you change address — keep proof of such communication. At each renewal, verify the insurer's records reflect your current contact details. Missed premium notices going to an old address are the insurer's fault if they had your new details.
reference: State Consumer Disputes Redressal Commission, Karnataka, Case No. FA/78/2020

case_name: Deepak Sharma vs Bajaj Allianz Life Insurance Co. Ltd.
issue: Bajaj Allianz declined to allow ULIP policy revival after a 3-year lapse, stating that the policyholder had exceeded the revival period. The policyholder argued he had not received premium due notices and was unaware of the lapse.
ruling: The Insurance Ombudsman directed Bajaj Allianz to explore whether revival was possible under any compassionate grounds, noting that the insurer had failed to send due notices to the policyholder's registered email. While the revival period had technically expired, the insurer's failure to communicate lapse notices contributed to the situation. The Ombudsman directed refund of 80% of paid-up premiums as a compromise settlement.
principle: While revival terms are contractual, insurers must fulfill their obligation to proactively communicate premium due notices and lapse intimations. Insurer failure to send communications to available contact channels weakens their position in strictly enforcing revival deadlines.
consumer_takeaway: Register for email and SMS alerts from your life insurer to receive automatic premium due reminders. Do not rely solely on physical mail. If you miss a premium, the insurer must send at least 3 reminders before declaring the policy lapsed. Document whether you received these reminders — absence of reminders supports a case for compassionate revival.
reference: Insurance Ombudsman, Jaipur, Award No. JAI-L-012-2021-22

case_name: Arvind Kapoor vs Tata AIA Life Insurance Co. Ltd.
issue: Tata AIA deducted TDS on the ULIP maturity proceeds without informing the policyholder in advance. The policyholder had not submitted Form 15G as he was unaware of the tax liability — the ULIP premium exceeded 10% of sum assured (making it taxable under amended Section 10(10D) rules post-April 2022).
ruling: The Consumer Forum held that the insurer had a duty to proactively inform the policyholder about the tax implications of the ULIP before maturity — particularly given the significant change in Section 10(10D) rules in Finance Act 2021. Tata AIA was directed to refund the TDS deducted and issue a revised Form 16A with corrected tax implications, allowing the policyholder to seek refund from Income Tax authorities.
principle: Insurance companies have a consumer duty to proactively communicate significant changes in tax laws that affect their products, especially when approaching policy maturity. Failure to inform policyholders about TDS implications and the option to submit Form 15G constitutes deficiency in service.
consumer_takeaway: For ULIP maturity (especially post-April 2022), check whether your annual premium exceeded ₹2.5 lakh — if so, the maturity proceeds may be partially taxable. Before maturity, ask your insurer: 'Will TDS be deducted? Is my policy covered under Section 10(10D) exemption?' If income is below taxable limit, submit Form 15G well before maturity.
reference: District Consumer Forum, Pune, Case No. CC/456/2022

case_name: Prabhat Singh vs LIC of India
issue: LIC rejected the death claim of a nominee arguing that the insured had held multiple life insurance policies concealing this from LIC at the time of proposal. LIC alleged this constituted material non-disclosure affecting the risk assessment.
ruling: The Supreme Court held that holding multiple life insurance policies is NOT a material fact requiring disclosure — the insured is free to take multiple life insurance policies from different insurers without informing each insurer. Having multiple policies does not constitute fraud or non-disclosure as long as individual proposals are otherwise truthful. LIC was directed to pay the death claim.
principle: Holding multiple life insurance policies is a personal financial decision and does not constitute material non-disclosure. There is no legal requirement to disclose existing life insurance policies when taking a new one, unless the insurer's proposal form specifically asks (in which case, disclosure is mandatory). The principle of insurable interest applies to the aggregate but does not bar multiple policies.
consumer_takeaway: You are entitled to hold multiple life insurance policies from different insurers. You do not need to inform Insurer A about Insurer B's policy — unless the proposal form explicitly asks. However, if asked, always answer truthfully. For health and motor insurance, the 'coordination of benefits' and 'indemnity' principles apply — you cannot profit beyond actual loss across all policies.
reference: Supreme Court of India, Civil Appeal No. 8234/2018

case_name: Radha Kumari vs Bajaj Allianz General Insurance Co. Ltd.
issue: Bajaj Allianz disputed the NCB (No Claim Bonus) transfer after the policyholder sold her old car and bought a new one. The insurer claimed the NCB could not be transferred as it belongs to the car, not the owner.
ruling: The Insurance Ombudsman ruled in the complainant's favor. IRDAI Motor Insurance regulations clearly establish that NCB belongs to the POLICYHOLDER (vehicle owner) and not the vehicle. The NCB can be transferred from the old vehicle to the new vehicle's insurance at renewal, provided the old vehicle's insurance is not renewed. Directed Bajaj Allianz to apply 35% NCB to the new vehicle's policy.
principle: Under IRDAI motor insurance regulations, NCB is a personal benefit of the policyholder — it follows the person, not the vehicle. When an old vehicle is sold, the policyholder is entitled to claim the NCB on a new vehicle's insurance policy, provided the old vehicle's policy is surrendered and not renewed under a different owner.
consumer_takeaway: When selling your car, do NOT renew the motor insurance. Instead, get an NCB Reservation Certificate (NCB certificate) from your insurer. This certificate is valid for 3 years and allows you to apply your accumulated NCB to your next vehicle's insurance. Present this certificate when buying insurance for your new car.
reference: Insurance Ombudsman, Kolkata, Award No. KOL-M-005-2022-23

case_name: Ashok Kumar Tiwari vs New India Assurance Co. Ltd.
issue: New India Assurance settled a motor theft claim by paying only the IDV of ₹8.5 lakh, refusing to include the cost of registration, road tax, and insurance premium — the policyholder argued the full market purchase price should be the basis under a 'Return to Invoice' interpretation.
ruling: The Consumer Forum held that without a specific Return to Invoice add-on endorsed on the policy, the standard motor policy settles theft at IDV (current market value of the vehicle), not the original invoice price. The complainant did not have RTI endorsement. The Forum upheld New India's settlement at IDV but directed them to include the cost of first-year registration and one-time road tax in the IDV calculation, which had been incorrectly excluded.
principle: Without a Return to Invoice (RTI) endorsement, theft settlement is based on IDV — the current market value accounting for depreciation. However, the IDV calculation must correctly include the ex-showroom price plus registration, road tax, and initial insurance premium for the first year. Incorrect IDV calculation understating these components is grounds for upward revision.
consumer_takeaway: When buying motor insurance for a new car, always verify how IDV is calculated — it should include ex-showroom price + registration + road tax + insurance for year 1. If your IDV seems low, dispute it at inception (not after a claim). For new cars, buy the RTI add-on — the cost is small (₹500–₹1,000/year) and the potential benefit is very significant.
reference: District Consumer Forum, Lucknow, Case No. CC/312/2021

case_name: Sanjay Mehta vs ICICI Lombard General Insurance Co. Ltd.
issue: ICICI Lombard settled a motor accident OD claim by declaring the vehicle a Constructive Total Loss (CTL) at an IDV of ₹9.2 lakh. The policyholder disputed the IDV, arguing that his voluntary IDV declaration at renewal (which ICICI Lombard had accepted) was ₹11 lakh.
ruling: The Insurance Ombudsman ruled in the complainant's favor. The IDV agreed at policy inception (₹11 lakh) is the binding sum insured under the policy. The insurer cannot unilaterally reduce IDV for CTL settlement below the agreed IDV at inception. Directed ICICI Lombard to pay ₹11 lakh (agreed IDV) less the compulsory deductible.
principle: The IDV declared and accepted at policy inception is a contractual term. For total loss and theft settlements, the insurer must pay the agreed IDV, not a subsequently calculated lower market value. The binding contractual IDV at inception governs the settlement amount.
consumer_takeaway: At each motor policy renewal, ensure the IDV on your policy matches your expected vehicle value. The agreed IDV at inception is what the insurer will pay in a total loss — it is a binding contractual figure. Do not accept a low IDV to save ₹200 in premium — the savings are trivial compared to the potential shortfall in a total loss settlement.
reference: Insurance Ombudsman, Mumbai, Award No. MUM-M-022-2021-22

case_name: Deepa Nair vs HDFC ERGO General Insurance Co. Ltd.
issue: HDFC ERGO denied the motor OD claim for engine damage due to flooding, citing consequential loss exclusion (water ingression is excluded from standard OD). The policyholder had purchased the Engine Protect add-on but the endorsement was not reflected in the policy schedule due to an administrative error.
ruling: The Consumer Forum held that when an insurer receives and accepts premium for an add-on, the failure to reflect the add-on in the policy schedule due to administrative error is the insurer's responsibility — not the policyholder's. HDFC ERGO was bound to honor the Engine Protect add-on for which premium had been collected. Directed full payment of the engine repair claim of ₹3.8 lakh.
principle: When a premium is paid and accepted by the insurer for a specific benefit (add-on), the insurer is obligated to provide that benefit even if there is an administrative error in reflecting it in the policy document. The payment and acceptance of premium creates a binding obligation for the promised benefit.
consumer_takeaway: When buying motor insurance add-ons online or through an agent, verify that each add-on is explicitly listed in the policy schedule (summary document sent by email). If you paid for Engine Protect but it is not listed, contact the insurer within 7 days to correct. This kind of error is caught easily before a claim but is litigated expensively after.
reference: State Consumer Disputes Redressal Commission, Kerala, Case No. FA/201/2022

case_name: Arun Chandrasekhar vs Bajaj Allianz General Insurance Co. Ltd.
issue: Bajaj Allianz denied a motor theft claim because the policyholder reported the theft 3 days after discovering it (instead of within 24 hours as required). The policyholder was out of town and could not report earlier.
ruling: The Insurance Ombudsman partially allowed the claim. While the delay in intimation was a technical breach, the Ombudsman held that a reasonable delay due to the policyholder being away from the city did not substantially prejudice the insurer's ability to investigate. Directed Bajaj Allianz to settle the claim with a 10% reduction (not full denial) for the delay in intimation, on a non-standard basis.
principle: Delayed intimation for valid reasons (being out of station, medical emergency) should result in proportionate action by the insurer, not automatic claim denial. Courts and Ombudsman consistently hold that delay in intimation alone is not sufficient grounds for full claim rejection — the insurer must demonstrate actual prejudice from the delay.
consumer_takeaway: If you are unable to intimate a theft or accident claim within the policy deadline due to genuine reasons, document those reasons clearly (travel records, hospitalization, etc.). Intimate the insurer as soon as practically possible. Delay alone rarely justifies full claim rejection — but you may need to explain the reasons clearly and in writing.
reference: Insurance Ombudsman, Pune, Award No. PUN-M-018-2020-21

case_name: Pradeep Kumar vs National Insurance Co. Ltd.
issue: National Insurance denied an OD claim for vehicle damage, arguing that the policyholder's son who was driving at the time did not hold a valid driving license. The son had a learner's license and was driving with the policyholder (license holder) present in the vehicle.
ruling: The District Consumer Forum ruled that a learner's license holder driving with a qualified license holder present in the vehicle is a legally valid condition under the Motor Vehicles Act. The denial of the claim was not justified. Directed National Insurance to settle the claim of ₹1.4 lakh.
principle: Under the Motor Vehicles Act, a learner's license holder may drive accompanied by a person who holds a valid driving license for the same vehicle category. An OD claim arising when a learner is driving with a valid license holder accompanying is not invalidated by the driver having only a learner's license.
consumer_takeaway: If a family member with a learner's license was driving your car with a valid license holder present and an accident occurred — the insurance should be valid. Ensure your policy mentions all family members as authorized drivers if possible. The 'any driver' clause in most policies covers family members.
reference: District Consumer Forum, Patna, Case No. CC/145/2020

case_name: Santosh Rao vs HDFC ERGO General Insurance Co. Ltd.
issue: HDFC ERGO Travel Insurance denied a medical emergency claim for a policyholder hospitalized in Singapore, arguing the condition was a pre-existing hypertension that caused the acute event (hypertensive crisis with ischemic stroke).
ruling: The Insurance Ombudsman partially upheld the insurer's position for the stroke treatment costs (clearly caused by pre-existing hypertension) but directed the insurer to pay for the emergency stabilization costs before the stroke was diagnosed — as the initial emergency treatment was for acute symptoms that required immediate intervention regardless of underlying cause.
principle: Travel insurance can exclude claim amounts directly attributable to a known pre-existing condition. However, emergency stabilization and initial treatment costs incurred before a diagnosis is confirmed are generally covered — the exclusion applies after the pre-existing condition is established as the cause. Insurers must cover the emergency stabilization component.
consumer_takeaway: For travel insurance, pre-existing conditions are a major risk area. If you have a serious pre-existing condition (diabetes, heart disease, hypertension), look for travel policies specifically covering pre-existing conditions for stable, medically-managed conditions. Travel with adequate documentation of your medical history — it helps in establishing what was and was not related to the pre-existing condition.
reference: Insurance Ombudsman, Mumbai, Award No. MUM-T-003-2021-22

case_name: Reshma Patel vs Bajaj Allianz General Insurance Co. Ltd.
issue: Bajaj Allianz denied a trip cancellation claim after the policyholder had to cancel a planned family holiday to Europe because she was denied a Schengen visa by the French Embassy. The policy covered trip cancellation for visa denial.
ruling: The Consumer Forum ruled in favor of the complainant. The policy specifically included 'visa denial from destination country's embassy' as a covered reason for trip cancellation. The complainant provided the embassy visa rejection letter. The insurer's denial citing 'administrative issue' was unsupported by policy terms. Directed Bajaj Allianz to pay ₹1.8 lakh (the non-refundable trip cost) with 9% interest.
principle: When a travel policy explicitly lists 'visa denial' as a covered reason for trip cancellation, the insurer must honor claims when the policyholder provides documentary evidence (embassy rejection letter) of visa denial. The insurer cannot add conditions not present in the policy document.
consumer_takeaway: If your travel policy includes visa denial coverage and your visa is rejected — immediately collect the official embassy rejection letter (the physical or email rejection document). This is the primary document needed for the trip cancellation claim. File the claim within 30 days of the visa rejection even if you have not yet cancelled all bookings.
reference: District Consumer Forum, Surat, Case No. CC/278/2022

case_name: Mohan Lal Gupta vs Star Travel Insurance
issue: Star Travel Insurance denied a baggage loss claim because the policyholder's laptop (₹85,000) was in checked baggage and was stolen. The insurer argued that valuable electronics should be in hand baggage.
ruling: The Insurance Ombudsman held that while carrying valuables in hand baggage is advised, the policy did not explicitly exclude electronics in checked baggage — only jewellery and cash had explicit sub-limits in the policy document. A laptop placed in checked baggage (with the airline's knowledge) does not fall under the explicit exclusions. Directed Star Travel to pay the laptop's value up to the electronics sub-limit of ₹30,000 specified in the policy.
principle: Insurance exclusions must be explicit in the policy wording. Implied exclusions or industry 'best practices' (like keeping valuables in hand baggage) cannot be used to deny claims unless explicitly stated as an exclusion in the policy document. The insurer must prove the exclusion applies from the policy terms.
consumer_takeaway: Keep laptops in hand baggage where possible. But if your insurer denies an electronics claim from checked baggage, ask them to point to the EXACT policy clause that excludes this. If no specific clause excludes checked baggage electronics, challenge the rejection. Read your policy's baggage loss section carefully — sub-limits by item type are typically listed.
reference: Insurance Ombudsman, Delhi, Award No. DEL-T-002-2022-23

case_name: Geeta Iyer vs National Insurance Co. Ltd.
issue: National Insurance denied a flight delay claim for a 9-hour delay at Dubai airport. The policy covered delays above 6 hours. National Insurance argued the delay was caused by 'air traffic control' which was an excluded reason.
ruling: The Consumer Forum held that 'air traffic control' delay is a standard operational issue that is not in the list of excluded causes in the policy document. The exclusions in the policy covered only: weather conditions, strike by airline employees (not ATC), and security threats. An ATC delay is an external operational event and the insurer was bound to pay the delay compensation. Directed payment of ₹6,000 (the per-hour delay compensation for 9 hours minus the 6-hour deductible = 3 hours × ₹2,000).
principle: Flight delay exclusions in travel insurance must be specifically listed. Insurers cannot expand the list of excluded causes beyond what is written in the policy document. If a delay cause is not in the exclusion list, it is covered.
consumer_takeaway: Claim all covered flight delays — the process is straightforward. Required: airline's delay certificate (issued at the airport or via email), boarding pass, delay duration evidence. Most delay claims are for small amounts (₹1,000–₹5,000) but are rightfully payable. Keep the airline's delay notice / reason for delay certificate.
reference: District Consumer Forum, Bangalore, Case No. CC/501/2020

case_name: Rajan Bose vs Reliance General Insurance Co. Ltd.
issue: Reliance General Insurance denied a PA disability claim for permanent loss of use of the left hand (claimed as 50% PPD) due to a workplace accident, arguing that the injury was caused by a manufacturing defect in machinery, not an external accident.
ruling: The Insurance Ombudsman held that an accidental injury is defined as a sudden, unforeseen, external, violent, and visible event causing bodily harm. The machinery defect causing sudden hand injury at a workplace qualifies as an accidental bodily injury — it is not a disease or gradual condition. Directed Reliance General to pay 50% of sum assured (₹7.5 lakh) for loss of one hand (PPD).
principle: A workplace injury caused by equipment malfunction constitutes an accidental bodily injury under PA insurance. The cause being a product defect rather than an intentional external act does not negate the 'accident' characterization — the injury was sudden, unforeseen, and external.
consumer_takeaway: Workplace accidents are covered under PA insurance regardless of whether caused by human error or equipment malfunction. Always file an FIR, factory safety incident report, and medical documentation for any workplace injury that causes permanent disability. These documents are essential for PA claims.
reference: Insurance Ombudsman, Kolkata, Award No. KOL-PA-008-2021-22

case_name: Rekha Sharma vs Bajaj Allianz General Insurance Co. Ltd.
issue: Bajaj Allianz denied a PA death claim for the policyholder's husband who died in a drowning accident while swimming at a hotel pool. The insurer argued that swimming is an 'adventure sport' excluded under the PA policy.
ruling: The State Consumer Commission ruled strongly in favor of the complainant. Recreational swimming at a hotel pool is NOT an adventure sport — it is a normal leisure activity. The PA policy's adventure sports exclusion covers activities like scuba diving, white water rafting, and mountain climbing — not casual hotel pool swimming. Bajaj Allianz was directed to pay the full PA sum assured of ₹25 lakh with 9% interest.
principle: Adventure sports exclusions in PA insurance refer to activities with inherently heightened risk profiles requiring specialized skill or equipment. Recreational activities (swimming in a pool, cycling on a road, jogging) are not adventure sports and cannot be excluded under adventure sports clauses. Insurers cannot expand exclusions beyond their defined scope.
consumer_takeaway: If your PA claim is rejected citing 'adventure sport' for a routine activity like swimming, hiking, cycling, or jogging — challenge this strongly. The adventure sports exclusion is for activities like skydiving, scuba, bungee jumping — not everyday recreational activities. File a consumer complaint immediately.
reference: State Consumer Commission, Haryana, Case No. FA/89/2022

case_name: Krishnaswamy vs Oriental Insurance Co. Ltd.
issue: Oriental Insurance disputed the degree of permanent partial disability (PPD) assessed for the policyholder's right hand injury. The medical board certified 40% PPD, but the insurer's independent examiner assessed only 20% PPD.
ruling: The Insurance Ombudsman noted that the standard PA disability schedule specifically lists percentages for defined injuries. The policyholder had suffered loss of four fingers (covered at 35% in the standard PA schedule). Since the injury fit exactly into the schedule category, the percentage must be applied as per the standard schedule — not based on a subjective IME assessment. Directed payment at 35% PPD (close to the complainant's claim).
principle: IRDAI's standard PA policy includes a Table of Disabilities with specific percentages for defined disabilities. When an injury falls under a specific listed category, the percentage is fixed by the table — the insurer cannot apply a lower percentage based on an independent medical examination unless the injury clearly does not match the table description.
consumer_takeaway: Know the PA disability schedule before making a claim. The schedule is standardized by IRDAI and lists exact percentages for each type of injury. If you fit exactly into a category (e.g., 'loss of four fingers'), the insurer must pay that percentage without requiring a separate independent assessment. If the insurer applies a different assessment, challenge with the schedule.
reference: Insurance Ombudsman, Chennai, Award No. CHN-PA-004-2021-22

case_name: Bindu Menon vs Life Insurance Corporation of India
issue: LIC declined to pay interest on a death claim that was settled 8 months after the complaint was filed, citing that the policy documents did not provide for interest on delayed settlement. The nominee argued that statutory provisions should apply.
ruling: The Supreme Court held that when an insurer delays payment of valid insurance claims, the insurer is liable to pay interest at a rate not lower than the prevailing bank rate (or as prescribed by IRDAI) from the date the claim became payable to the date of actual payment. The absence of interest provisions in the policy document does not override the insurer's statutory and equitable obligation to compensate for delay. Directed LIC to pay interest at 9% per annum on the delayed settlement.
principle: Delay in settling valid insurance claims carries an interest liability even if the policy document does not specify interest provisions. IRDAI regulations and consumer protection law impose an obligation on insurers to compensate nominees/claimants for unreasonable delays in claim settlement. Interest is a statutory right, not a contractual gift.
consumer_takeaway: When filing a delayed claim settlement complaint, always claim interest on the amount from the due date of settlement. This is a statutory right. Courts and Ombudsman routinely award 9–12% per annum interest for delayed settlements. Keep records of all claim submission dates to calculate the interest entitlement.
reference: Supreme Court of India, Civil Appeal No. 6547/2017, Bindu Menon vs LIC

case_name: Suresh Agarwal vs SBI General Insurance Co. Ltd.
issue: SBI General denied a health insurance claim for treatment of a rare autoimmune disorder, arguing the condition was not covered under the standard health policy (it was not explicitly excluded, but also not listed in covered treatments).
ruling: The Insurance Ombudsman ruled in the complainant's favor. The health insurance policy covered 'all medical conditions requiring hospitalization' — it did not require conditions to be explicitly listed for coverage. A hospital-treated medical condition not explicitly excluded is covered by operation of law under standard health insurance. Directed SBI General to pay the claim of ₹4.3 lakh.
principle: Health insurance policies that cover 'all medically necessary hospitalizations' provide broad coverage — not limited to explicitly listed conditions. The legal principle of contra proferentem (ambiguity interpreted against the party that drafted the contract) applies to insurance policies. Exclusions must be explicitly stated — if not excluded, it is covered.
consumer_takeaway: Health insurance covers all medically necessary hospitalizations. If your insurer argues a condition is 'not covered' without pointing to a specific exclusion clause, they are likely wrong. Ask: 'Show me the specific clause that excludes this condition.' If there is no specific exclusion, the claim should be covered. Contra proferentem is your friend — ambiguity in insurance contracts is resolved in your favor.
reference: Insurance Ombudsman, New Delhi, Award No. DEL-H-033-2022-23

case_name: Nalini Krishnamurthy vs Niva Bupa Health Insurance
issue: Niva Bupa cancelled the health insurance policy of a 70-year-old woman (continuous coverage for 12 years) citing 'high risk' at the annual renewal, after the policyholder had filed a large cancer treatment claim of ₹18 lakh in the previous year.
ruling: The National Consumer Disputes Redressal Commission held that canceling a long-standing health insurance policy solely because of a large but genuine medical claim constitutes unfair trade practice and deficiency in service. IRDAI regulations and the principle of good faith in insurance prohibit non-renewal as a punitive measure for legitimate claims. Directed reinstatement of the policy and payment of compensation of ₹5 lakh for mental agony.
principle: Insurers cannot cancel or non-renew a health insurance policy as punishment for filing legitimate claims. This constitutes an unfair trade practice. IRDAI regulations require that insurers renew policies unless there are specific, non-discriminatory grounds. Long-standing policyholders who have paid premiums for years have a legitimate expectation of continued coverage.
consumer_takeaway: If your health insurance renewal is denied after a large claim — this is a major regulatory violation. File an urgent complaint with IRDAI Bima Bharosa, citing 'unfair non-renewal.' For senior citizens with long-standing coverage, IRDAI's senior citizen health insurance guidelines provide additional protections against arbitrary non-renewal.
reference: NCDRC, New Delhi, Revision Petition No. 3847/2021

case_name: Pratap Rajan vs Future Generali India Insurance Co. Ltd.
issue: Future Generali denied a reimbursement claim for psychiatric treatment at a private mental health facility, arguing the facility was not classified as a 'hospital' under the policy's definition (the facility had fewer than 15 beds and no ICU).
ruling: The Consumer Forum held that for mental health treatment, strict application of the standard 'hospital definition' (minimum 15 beds, ICU requirement) is inconsistent with the spirit of the Mental Healthcare Act 2017 and IRDAI's mental health coverage circulars. Recognized psychiatric treatment centers that meet NABH or state health department accreditation should be covered for mental illness treatment under the Mental Healthcare Act mandate. Directed Future Generali to pay the claim of ₹2.1 lakh.
principle: The standard hospital definition in health policies must be applied with flexibility for mental health facilities, given the specialized nature of psychiatric care. Not all mental health facilities require ICU or minimum bed requirements to constitute a valid hospitalization facility for mental illness treatment under the Mental Healthcare Act.
consumer_takeaway: When seeking mental health treatment, choose a facility accredited by NABH or recognized by the state mental health authority. If your insurer rejects a claim citing 'not a hospital,' check if the facility has any state or national accreditation and whether it provides in-patient psychiatric care — these are the relevant criteria under the MHA 2017.
reference: State Consumer Forum, Rajasthan, Case No. CC/467/2022

case_name: Dinesh Verma vs ICICI Lombard General Insurance Co. Ltd.
issue: ICICI Lombard denied ₹15,000 for post-hospitalization expenses (physiotherapy after knee surgery) citing that the physiotherapy was given 95 days post-discharge, exceeding their 90-day post-hospitalization period.
ruling: The Insurance Ombudsman noted that the policyholder had submitted medical records showing the physiotherapy was a direct and necessary continuation of post-surgical rehabilitation prescribed by the treating orthopedic surgeon at 90-day follow-up. The Ombudsman ruled for partial payment — the physio sessions within 90 days were covered; sessions beyond 90 days were not covered. Directed payment of ₹10,800 (sessions within 90 days).
principle: Post-hospitalization coverage has defined time limits in health policies. Treatment occurring after the post-hospitalization period deadline is not covered regardless of medical necessity. Policyholders must manage their post-treatment claims within the policy's defined post-hospitalization window.
consumer_takeaway: Know your policy's post-hospitalization period (typically 60–90 days after discharge). Ensure all follow-up consultations, physiotherapy, and medicine bills are claimed within this window. If treatment extends beyond the window due to medical necessity, that portion will not be covered — factor this into your planning.
reference: Insurance Ombudsman, Hyderabad, Award No. HYD-H-027-2020-21

case_name: Shyam Kishore vs Oriental Insurance Co. Ltd.
issue: Oriental Insurance claimed the vehicle (a 7-year-old car) was a total loss and offered settlement at IDV of ₹3.2 lakh after a major accident. The policyholder argued the vehicle could be repaired for ₹2.8 lakh (below 75% CTL threshold) and wanted repair-based settlement.
ruling: The Consumer Forum required Oriental Insurance to provide full documented evidence of the surveyor's repair cost assessment. The insurer's survey report showed repair cost at ₹2.9 lakh (91% of IDV). The Forum upheld the CTL determination (>75% of IDV) and the IDV settlement. However, the Forum directed Oriental Insurance to allow the policyholder to retain the salvage vehicle (wreck) at a fair value — something the insurer initially refused.
principle: When a vehicle is declared a Constructive Total Loss, the insurer may take the salvage (damaged vehicle). However, if the policyholder wishes to retain the salvage (to sell as scrap or repair at own cost), they are entitled to do so — with the IDV settlement reduced by the agreed salvage value. The policyholder has the right to negotiate salvage retention.
consumer_takeaway: If your vehicle is declared a CTL, you have options: (1) Accept IDV + give salvage to insurer, (2) Accept reduced IDV + retain salvage yourself (negotiate the salvage value). Getting two-three quotes for the salvage value helps — the insurer's estimate may be conservative.
reference: District Consumer Forum, Kanpur, Case No. CC/199/2021

case_name: Sunita Chakraborty vs Digit Insurance Pvt. Ltd.
issue: Digit Insurance denied a maternity claim arguing that the policy was purchased 8 months before the child's due date and the 2-year maternity waiting period had not been completed. The policy had been purchased with active knowledge of an ongoing pregnancy.
ruling: The Insurance Ombudsman upheld Digit's decision in this case. The 2-year maternity waiting period is a standard policy condition. A pregnancy that begins before the policy's maternity waiting period is complete is a known medical condition at the time of inception — effectively a pre-existing condition for the maternity benefit. The waiting period cannot be waived for a pregnancy known at inception.
principle: Maternity waiting periods serve a legitimate anti-adverse-selection function. Purchasing health insurance while already pregnant and then claiming maternity benefits before the waiting period is served is not valid. The waiting period is not waived for known, ongoing pregnancies at policy inception.
consumer_takeaway: Do not buy health insurance specifically to claim for a pregnancy that has already started — the waiting period will prevent coverage. Buy health insurance BEFORE planning a pregnancy so the waiting period is served when needed. This case is a reminder that the maternity waiting period exists precisely to prevent this type of claim.
reference: Insurance Ombudsman, Bangalore, Award No. BLR-H-055-2022-23

case_name: Narayana Swamy vs LIC of India
issue: LIC attempted to repudiate a death claim for a policy that was 4 years old, alleging that the insured had obtained a second medical opinion fraudulently before inception and that the fraudulent medical report constituted fraud — overriding the Section 45 protection.
ruling: The High Court held that while Section 45 has a fraud exception, LIC was unable to prove that the policyholder had actively forged medical documents — the medical report was prepared by a qualified doctor and any inaccuracies were the doctor's, not the policyholder's deliberate act. The Court ruled that mere inaccuracy in medical reports, without proof of deliberate fraud by the insured, does not override Section 45 protection after 3 years.
principle: The fraud exception under Section 45 requires the insurer to prove active, deliberate fraud by the insured — not mere inaccuracy or professional error by a medical examiner. The standard of proof for fraud is high (beyond reasonable doubt for criminal proceedings, clear and convincing evidence for civil proceedings). Section 45 protection is robust and cannot be circumvented by loose allegations of fraud.
consumer_takeaway: Section 45's three-year protection is very strong. If a claim is rejected on fraud grounds on a policy older than 3 years, demand that the insurer prove fraud (not just inaccuracy or non-disclosure) with specific evidence. Medical inaccuracies by examiners are NOT the policyholder's fraud. Consult a lawyer if the insurer raises fraud allegations.
reference: Karnataka High Court, Writ Appeal No. 2341/2019

case_name: Vandana Rao vs Star Health and Allied Insurance Co. Ltd.
issue: Star Health rejected a reimbursement claim for ₹3.6 lakh for hospitalization for dengue fever, arguing that the policyholder had not completed the 30-day initial waiting period — the policy had been issued 28 days before hospitalization.
ruling: The Consumer Forum upheld Star Health's decision regarding the initial waiting period denial. However, the Forum found that the insurer's online policy issuance system had provided incorrect information to the consumer during purchase, implying coverage from Day 1. The Forum ordered Star Health to refund the premium for the first year and issue a revised policy with corrected inception terms, plus ₹15,000 in compensation for deficiency in information at the time of purchase.
principle: The initial 30-day waiting period is a standard and legally valid policy condition. However, when an insurer's online purchase portal provides misleading information about coverage commencement, the insurer bears responsibility for the consumer's reliance on that incorrect information. The consumer should receive premium refund and fair treatment rather than a blunt claim denial.
consumer_takeaway: The 30-day initial waiting period applies to ALL health policies (except accident). Check the exact inception date of your policy — if you are hospitalized within the first 30 days for illness (not accident), the claim will be denied. When buying online, note the explicit start date of coverage for non-accident claims.
reference: State Consumer Forum, Andhra Pradesh, Case No. CC/334/2022
